speaker: SS
words: SCHRAGER: The first thing I was going to ask you about Grandfather

speaker: SS
words: Lieuallen. And I thought I'd ask you first off if you know how he

speaker: SS
words: happened to come to Idaho?

speaker: SS
words: LILLIAN WOOPWORTH OTNESS:  I don't know very much about it. I know that he

speaker: SS
words: belonged to a family that had moved west and had settled around Athena

speaker: SS
words: and Weston, Oregon. And several of them came up here, according to

speaker: SS
words: family tradition at least, to look over the location. And the others

speaker: SS
words: were farmers, he didn't care for that, he liked the cattle ranching.

speaker: SS
words: And they came up here, and of course you've heard all the stories about

speaker: SS
words: how the grass was nigh and all over the hills and so on, and he thought

speaker: SS
words: that this would be a wonderful place to raise cattle. And so he came up

speaker: SS
words: and first, I guess, was on a tract out near what I know as Munson farm

speaker: SS
words: out in the Robinson Lake area. And brought a herd of cattle up here, and

speaker: SS
words: in one of the hard winters, what was it. . .

speaker: SS
words: I'm not sure, I think it was around '73 or '74.

speaker: LO
words: Something like that. I think maybe that's about right. And they had

speaker: LO
words: heavy snows and the ground froze, and the cattle couldn't get to the feed

speaker: LO
words: and he lost forty-thousand dollars worth of cattle, which was a goodly sum

speaker: LO
words: in those days. And apparently it was about that time that he decided

speaker: LO
words: to try something else—the store and that sort of thing.

speaker: SS
words: So he was probably one of the main stockmen in the country, if not the

speaker: SS
words: main one, when he came that herd.

speaker: LO
words: That might be, I just don't know.

speaker: SS
words: You know when they say that in the History of Ndrth Idaho that he was the

speaker: SS
words: first man to settle in the valley, Paradise Valley, do you know if that's

speaker: SS
words: so or not?

speaker: LO
words: I really don't.

speaker: SS
words: The summer of 1871. . .

speaker: LO
words: Yea, that1 was the time that my mother always said that the family came.

speaker: LO
words: But I really don't know, he was one of the early ones, certainly. And at

speaker: LO
words: one time he and Russell and McGregor and Deakin owned property that adjoined

speaker: LO
words: at Sixth and Main Street—it was a four corner place. And he had the

speaker: LO
words: property west of ^ain and north of Sixth at that time. Now I suppose that

speaker: LO
words: was a homestead that he took over from somebody.

speaker: SS
words: Did neafor, was it Neff that he got it from? This is what I've heard, I

speaker: SS
words: don't.

speaker: LO
words: I don't Rnow whether he got all that land from Neff or not,or whether it

speaker: LO
words: was just the store. I thought from what mother had told me that it was the

speaker: LO
words: store he acquired from Neff and the post office went along with it. But

speaker: LO
words: I don't know whether Neff had the homestead or not.

speaker: SS
words: But do you think the reason that he went into town and started a store

speaker: SS
words: was because of that hard winter, probably?

speaker: LO
words: I think so, that's what I've understood from my mother as the reason. And

speaker: LO
words: apparently he was very public spirited and civic-minded and interested in

speaker: LO
words: developing the town. And was busy in whatever was going on that way.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I wonder if the town was just his store to start with?

speaker: LO
words: I think it was a little more than that oh, maybe to start with, but later

speaker: LO
words: on the store was located at First and Main, y'know where the Bank of Idaho

speaker: LO
words: is now, and White's Drug Store was across the way—you've seen those

speaker: LO
words: pictures with that. Dr.  had his dental office in that same block

speaker: LO
words: too, north of First Street. I heard my mother tell about how she used to

speaker: LO
words: play with Margaret McP alley. They lived there within about a half block

speaker: LO
words: of each other. This was while my mother was still living there, on First

speaker: LO
words: before they built the house on Almon Street. Before that, she told me once

speaker: LO
words: they'd been in a house that was located where the old post office is

speaker: LO
words: now.

speaker: SS
words: Wheny had the store, did they live right by it? Or was it a cabin?

speaker: LO
words: Well, I had the impression that it was very close there, and Mother used

speaker: LO
words: to be in the store quite a bit, I guess, as a little girl, and she told Me

speaker: LO
words: about how the Indian women would come in and they'd be so fascinated with

speaker: LO
words: her red hair. They'd want to hold her and play with her because she

speaker: LO
words: had red hair, and she was pretty small at that time, must have been.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember anything else that she said about what the store was like?

speaker: LO
words: No, she didn't say a great deal about what the store was like. This also

speaker: LO
words: was during the time when they had the Indian scare and they had Fort

speaker: LO
words: Russell, y'know, but I think she was only about three years old then. Wasn't

speaker: LO
words: that in '77?

speaker: SS
words: That's what Homer David says too, that she was in the fort.

speaker: LO
words: Yes,well she has told me about that—about how people in general would

speaker: LO
words: go and work their farms during the daytime and then go hole up in the

speaker: LO
words: fort at night. She would go with her mother, now I don't know whether,

speaker: LO
words: oh yes by that time they must have been in town, I don't think they

speaker: LO
words: were on that farm any longer. And her mother would hitch up the wagon

speaker: LO
words: at the end of the day and take the children up to the fort. They'd go

speaker: LO
words: in there and stay overnight. But that didn't seem to last very long.

speaker: SS
words: So they spent their days on the farm—the women and children did too.

speaker: LO
words: Un hum.

speaker: SS
words: Did she remember at all what it was like in the fort? Wh± she did in

speaker: SS
words: the fort?

speaker: LO
words: Not very much. She didn't remember very much of that. Somebody from

speaker: LO
words: Spokane wanted to come down and interview her about that and she discouraged

speaker: LO
words: because she said she was just too young to have remembered very much

speaker: LO
words: about it.

speaker: SS
words: Three or four is probably about the threshold of memory.

speaker: LO
words: Um hum, urn hum. No, she didn't seem to have very clear recollections of

speaker: LO
words: that and I can certahly understand it .

speaker: SS
words: What about this question of the naming of Moscow as you understand it?

speaker: SS
words: I actually read in that History of North Idaho, I was looking at the other

speaker: SS
words: day, they gave the credit for deciding to name the town Moscow.

speaker: LO
words: Well, it seems to have been the object of considerable specdation, and so

speaker: LO
words: on. I think Grandfather probably was very influential, was right in the

speaker: LO
words: business of planning on that. Mother sort of changed her stories as the

speaker: LO
words: years went by. For years she told me that the reason they chose Moscow

speaker: LO
words: was that they wanted a name that no other towns in the United States

speaker: LO
words: Or maybe I misunderstood it—maybe they just didn't want one that was very

speaker: LO
words: close because apparently Neff had come from near Moscow, Pennsylvania.Then

speaker: LO
words: later on Mother told me that they also wanted something that sounded citified

speaker: LO
words: and glamourous, and that Moscow, Russia filled the bill as far as that

speaker: LO
words: was concerned.

speaker: SS
words: Did she have any opinion about the question of Neff's flaming the town

speaker: SS
words: after his hometown.when she talked about it?

speaker: LO
words: I'm trying to remember. It seems the way she used to tell it, they reached

speaker: LO
words: a consensus on it, and of course they had called it Hog Heaven beforehand

speaker: LO
words: and the women objected to that because they ttought that was not a very

speaker: LO
words: dignified name for a beautiful valley like this. So they discarded Hog

speaker: LO
words: Heaven and they wanted to call it Paradise, but I think the post office wouldn't

speaker: LO
words: approve that because there were ether towns that had that same name. She

speaker: LO
words: was very definite about the fact that it had nothing to do with Russians

speaker: LO
words: among the population. Somebody had written a book—Mother said when he

speaker: LO
words: was old and senile and said that that was the reason that there were

speaker: LO
words: Russians that justwould come down out of the hills occasionally and then

speaker: LO
words: disappear back into the hills, and that they were responsible for naming

speaker: LO
words: it Moscow. She was very positive about that fact that that wasn't true.

speaker: LO
words: And my father, when he was county auditor, did a good deal of checking

speaker: LO
words: with the county records. And he said there just were no Russian names

speaker: LO
words: around Moscow. Mother always felt that the reason that theory got

speaker: LO
words: accepted was because it appeared in print, and that it wasn't reliable at

speaker: LO
words: all.

speaker: SS
words: I don't know why there's such a controversy over the naming of Moscow,

speaker: SS
words: but there really seems to be.

speaker: LO
words: Well,and so many theories. I have a clipping some place that somebody

speaker: LO
words: had given to Mother and she just pooh-poohed the whole thing. It had

speaker: LO
words: to do with somebody pointing to my grandfather's beard and saying,

speaker: LO
words: "You look like a Russian with that long long beard, why don't we name

speaker: LO
words: it Moscow" And my mother thought that was perfectly ridiculous—there

speaker: LO
words: was just nothing to that.

speaker: SS
words: Y'know on this Hog Heaven, was that supposed to be because they ran hogs

speaker: SS
words: in the valley.

speaker: LO
words: Well, hogs liked to dig the camas, has been my understanding of the

speaker: LO
words: tradition and grew very fat on the camas root. And of course, there

speaker: LO
words: no question that there was camas around here. The men called it Hog

speaker: LO
words: Heaven because it was so good for the stock.

speaker: SS
words: What about the Indians that used to camp here in the early days? Did

speaker: SS
words: you ever hear about them from your mother?

speaker: LO
words: Not very much, she used to mention the Indains coming into the store, and

speaker: LO
words: of course she was aware of the fact that the Indian trails had run

speaker: LO
words: through here, but she didn't have very much to say about it. In fact,

speaker: LO
words: I can't remember her mentioning them particularly except in regard to her

speaker: LO
words: red hair.

speaker: SS
words: What about what Mr.Lieuallen sold mostly and the kind of business that

speaker: SS
words: he had in those days? Have you gleaned anything from looking at those

speaker: SS
words: ledgers that he had?

speaker: LO
words: Well, yes, I think it was pretty generalfwhat you'd expect of a country

speaker: LO
words: store. You know, tools that were commonly used, and produce, and staples

speaker: LO
words: 4nd apparently he was taking produce in trade, taking eggs and vegetables

speaker: LO
words: that people bought in and they got credit for and so on. Oh, fabrics,

speaker: LO
words: and hats, and shoes, and just the whole gamut of what you'd supply if

speaker: LO
words: you were running a store in a little frontier town.

speaker: SS
words: I read that he had to go to Walla Walla to get the first stock that he

speaker: SS
words: laid in, but I'm curious about where he would have continued to get his

speaker: SS
words: stock after that time. I wonder if he had to keep going back to Walla Walla

speaker: SS
words: since it was before the railroad came in?

speaker: LO
words: He probably did. I don't remember hearing Mother talk about that very

speaker: LO
words: much. She did talk about how he banked up at St. John because there

speaker: LO
words: wasn't a bank here in Moscow. And he would ride horseback up to St. John,

speaker: LO
words: and when he was going was rather carefully guarded secret because he'd

speaker: LO
words: be carrying gold and didn't want to get waylaid. But I don't remember

speaker: LO
words: that she ever said very much about where he got his stock.

speaker: SS
words: Have you heard the story that when the mail first came, it was left

speaker: SS
words: deposited there in a shoe box in the store. I guess I read that someplace.

speaker: SS
words: He started with a shoebox.

speaker: LO
words: Yes, I think I have. Of course most of the early post offices were some

speaker: LO
words: thing similar to that. Mail would be left in somebody's home or in a

speaker: LO
words: business, something like that, that may well be true about the shoe box.

speaker: SS
words: And then he sold his store and turned to selling land and platted

speaker: SS
words: the town of Moscow?

speaker: LO
words: Yes, that was it. He had financial reverses. Somebody absconded with

speaker: LO
words: money from a local bank and with a lot of records, a lot of things that

speaker: LO
words: had been left there for safe keeping, including a great meny notes that my

speaker: LO
words: from

speaker: LO
words: grandfather had taken people who owed him money. And without the notes

speaker: LO
words: he couldn't collect. So that, according to family tradition, he lost a

speaker: LO
words: good deal of money that way add was forced to make a choice between trying

speaker: LO
words: to maintain the store and trying to sell real estate and do that. And

speaker: LO
words: because he was so interested in building up the town he chose to do

speaker: LO
words: that and gave up the store.

speaker: SS
words: Have you heard anything about this business of him platting the town and

speaker: SS
words: then selling land?

speaker: LO
words: No, but I imagine that that is true because Lieuallen's addition

speaker: LO
words: is one of the sections of Moscow, and Mrs. Lieuallen, I think

speaker: LO
words: whether

speaker: LO
words: he was involved in the early platjfcing of the town. Now it extended to

speaker: LO
words: other parts of the town besides what he owned I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: I don't know, but I thought the book said actually he platted the town

speaker: SS
words: of Moscow in 1880.

speaker: LO
words: He may have.

speaker: SS
words: Did you tell me there was something about him giving free lots to people?

speaker: LO
words: That's what my mother told me—that he would give lots free to people

speaker: LO
words: if they would build houses on them. This was a device for promoting

speaker: LO
words: building up the town. Of course at that time lots weren't very expensive

speaker: LO
words: anyhow. Lots down along Asbury and Lieuallen street probably sold for

speaker: LO
words: thirty-five dollars, but he was interested in getting people to stay here

speaker: LO
words: and build homes for themselves.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know anything about his political convictions?

speaker: LO
words: No, I don't. I think Mother told me he was a Democrat. And having

speaker: LO
words: come from Tennessee, that could well be. But I don't know whether

speaker: LO
words: his convictions were very strong.

speaker: SS
words: Was it he that you told me that didn't care for his Black Republican

speaker: SS
words: cherries ?

speaker: LO
words: No, that was my other grandfather—that was my grandfather Woodworth who

speaker: LO
words: was a very strong Democrat. And he had this cherry tree in his orchard

speaker: LO
words: and he loved everything about the cherries except the name—Black Republican,

speaker: SS
words: Can you tell m e overall what you understand your grandfather's being like?

speaker: SS
words: What kind of person he was that's come down through history anyway as '

speaker: SS
words: the founding father of the town?

speaker: LO
words: Only that he seemed to be mixed up in everything that was goingpn. He

speaker: LO
words: seemed to be involved in everything that was developmental or innovative

speaker: LO
words: in the town. But beyond that I don't have any very strong impressions

speaker: LO
words: because I never knew either one of my Lieuallen grandparents.

speaker: SS
words: Are there other things that your mother told you about growing up in

speaker: SS
words: Moscow when she was a young person, that you remember?

speaker: LO
words: She went to Portland to school before there was a high school here. She

speaker: LO
words: went to St. Helen's Hall in Portland, and I suppose she must have gone

speaker: LO
words: on the train—by that time the railroad would have been in here, wouldn't

speaker: LO
words: it? She was born in '74.

speaker: SS
words: She'd be eleven.

speaker: LO
words: Uh huh. And I don't know how many years she went down there,

speaker: LO
words: but I know that she did go down there to boarding

speaker: LO
words: school. And then when the high school was started here, she attended the

speaker: LO
words: high school. And I thought for years that she had graduated from high

speaker: LO
words: school here, but it turned out that she had married my father in January

speaker: LO
words: before she was due to graduate in May, so she didn't actually finish

speaker: LO
words: high school. Oh, I think there was a lot of activity with the young

speaker: LO
words: people—that is, Mother belonged to a mandolin club, and she played the

speaker: LO
words: piano and the mandolin club apparently had quite a membership. £ have

speaker: LO
words: pictures of the members of the mandolin club—there must have been, oh,

speaker: LO
words: maybe ten, twelve mandolin players. They used to play around for various

speaker: LO
words: social events. I think like any small town, they made their own fun,

speaker: LO
words: their social life.

speaker: SS
words: She didn't go to college then?

speaker: LO
words: No, in those days married women didn't go to college. They didn't even

speaker: LO
words: go to college when I was in schoolj It was unusual for any married women

speaker: LO
words: to be in college, and the ones that were, we thought were sort of odd. I

speaker: LO
words: suppose when I was in college, I knew maybe half a dozen. And this was just.

speaker: SS
words: What would you think would be odd about it?

speaker: LO
words: Well, married women stayed home and took care of their families. It seems

speaker: LO
words: ridiculous now that it would be odd, but even the ones that I knew in

speaker: LO
words: college mostly were wives of staff members who somehow or another hadn't

speaker: LO
words: completed their education. And I knew two or three that finished up here.

speaker: SS
words: Single women though—it was completely acceptable for them to go to college

speaker: SS
words: in you mother's day?

speaker: LO
words: Oh, I don't know about that. Yes, it was acceptable, but there just

speaker: LO
words: weren't very many of them that did. There weren't very many men that

speaker: LO
words: did either. When they first started the University, they ran a preparatory

speaker: LO
words: school because the students that came were not really high school graduates

speaker: LO
words: a lot of them. And there was a lot of talk when I was a child about

speaker: LO
words: the prep school. It was discontinued after a while, when they started

speaker: LO
words: getting enough students that were prepared, but when it first started out

speaker: LO
words: the prep school was an important part of it.

speaker: SS
words: Was the prep school actually a high school?

speaker: LO
words: No, but I think it was sort of remedial courses for those who hadn't

speaker: SS
words: finished high school and who needed more background. And people came

speaker: SS
words: from other parts of the state to go to that before going to the university?

speaker: LO
words: That's my understanding. They'd do prep school work and then go on and

speaker: LO
words: finish their college. I don't know how long that went on. But it was a

speaker: LO
words: common term in the community.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think they were taught by the college teachers or had a separate

speaker: SS
words: faculty of their own, do you know that?

speaker: SS
words: The prep school? I don't know, I just don't know abut that.

speaker: SS
words: Now, your uncle, that was your mother's brother, he went to the college?

speaker: SS
words: Uh huh, uh huh. He was enrolled—the one that was the athlete.

speaker: SS
words: Yea.

speaker: SS
words: Yes he was a student in college, but not for very long because he died

speaker: SS
words: very young. And he would have been the only one of my mother's family.

speaker: SS
words: She had a brother and sister who died very young.

speaker: SS
words: Was that sickness?

speaker: LO
words: Oh, I think it was probably the diphtheria epidemic. I don't know how

speaker: LO
words: old they lived to be—I'd have to lo«k up the family records on that not old. And she

speaker: LO
words: and this brother were the only ones that grew up at ail.

speaker: SS
words: What- did you hear about his prowess as an athlete?

speaker: LO
words: Well, apparently he was a very good athlete. He was a baseball player,

speaker: LO
words: and he was very good in track. He got lots of medals in track meets, and

speaker: LO
words: so on. I doubt if he was around when they started having football.

speaker: LO
words: I don't know just when that was, but I don't think he was involved in

speaker: LO
words: that—but lots of baseball. I have medals that he won in intercollegiate

speaker: LO
words: track meets.

speaker: LO
words: You know Mrs. Bush was one of Governor McConnell's daughters. And she

speaker: LO
words: lived up on the hill there, kind of yards back to back with the Davids,

speaker: LO
words: the Homer Davids, and the Davids had four hoys. One time she was out

speaker: LO
words: in her back yard and she heard some of the David boys, I don't know which

speaker: LO
words: ones, talking on the other side of the fence. And they were trying to

speaker: LO
words: say the dirtiest words that they knew. And one of them said, "Oh, I know

speaker: LO
words: a good one—maure, manure!" And after a while another one said, "Oh, I know

speaker: LO
words: a better one—corset coverI" Now you remember that Sam, when you want something

speaker: LO
words: really pornographic

speaker: SS
words: Yea. (laughs) Things sure must have been different for that to be the

speaker: SS
words: dirtiest word they could think of.

speaker: LO
words: (laughs) Do you know what corset covers were? It was a kind of camisole

speaker: LO
words: that women wore under their dresses—well, it was the top to the petticoats,

speaker: LO
words: except petticoats in those days were probably separate. And the corset

speaker: LO
words: covers would tuck in under them and sometimes oh you know they had lace

speaker: LO
words: and insertion and ribbon and that sort of thing.

speaker: SS
words: You know I have to think about it—I really don't know what corset covers

speaker: SS
words: are, I mean I figured what they'd be but. . .

speaker: LO
words: Well, camisole is another name for it, but it was really going a long way

speaker: LO
words: to find a dirty word. Even'manure.'

speaker: SS
words: Did you know the McConnell daughters?

speaker: LO
words: Yea, I knew Carrie, well slightly. They were of my mother's generation.

speaker: LO
words: I knew Carrie quite well because she lived here all her life, I guess,

speaker: LO
words: and was a friend of my mother's for a long, long time. And Lived within

speaker: LO
words: a block of Mother during Mother's last fifteen, twenty years that she

speaker: LO
words: lived here in town. The others, Mamie Borah and Ollie Lu«ldbman, I met

speaker: LO
words: occasionally but I didn't knaw them well at all.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever hear much about what the social life used to be like around

speaker: SS
words: the mansion when they were girls? I've heard that it was really quite

speaker: SS
words: something from Lola Clyde,that.

speaker: LO
words: No, I don't think I'd ever heard very much about that.

speaker: SS
words: I guess maybe it was more that the Mcfbnnell daughters were sort of the

speaker: SS
words: center of Moscow society when they were young.

speaker: LO
words: That may well be.

speaker: SS
words: 0. K. now what's the story about your brother Jack's run in with the

speaker: SS
words: president of the university?

speaker: LO
words: My Uncle Jack.

speaker: SS
words: That's right, I mean your uncle.

speaker: LO
words: According to Mother he had been competing in these track meets and he was

speaker: LO
words: one of the successful ones, and the University of Idaho had a track meet

speaker: LO
words: scheduled with Washington Agricultural College at Pullman. And the rivalry

speaker: LO
words: was quite strong, and Idaho had been beating the ag college. President

speaker: LO
words: Gault called my uncle in and said that in the interest of good relations

speaker: LO
words: between the two colleges he didn't want the University of Idaho to win

speaker: LO
words: so big. So he wanted my uncle to see that he didn't win too many events.

speaker: LO
words: And my uncle said he didn't play that way—he played to win and he would

speaker: LO
words: feel he had to do his best and win or lose as he could. President Gault

speaker: LO
words: said that if he disobeyed him he would expell him from school. And

speaker: LO
words: he called, according to Mother's story, he called my grandfather and told

speaker: LO
words: him that if Jack didn't agree to do this that he would expell him from

speaker: LO
words: school. Jack refused to agree to do it and he did expell him from school.

speaker: SS
words: think Jack got to run the races? (laughs)

speaker: LO
words: I don't think he got to take part In that meet—I could be wrong about

speaker: LO
words: that, but I think he was not allowed to compete.

speaker: SS
words: Now that's something that wouldn't get very far these days either. I mean

speaker: SS
words: his father would probably be down there right now talking to the president

speaker: SS
words: of the university.

speaker: LO
words: Urn hum, probably. But in those days of course administrators had a

speaker: LO
words: good deal more power over individuals than they do now—that is it was

speaker: LO
words: a kind of dictatrship—you either shaped up or you couldn't stay in school.

speaker: SS
words: I'm really curious about what the university was like back

speaker: SS
words: in the early days. I guess there weren't very many students at all when

speaker: SS
words: your uncle was going there.

speaker: LO
words: Oh, I don't imagine so. I don't imagine there would have been very many.

speaker: LO
words: And for many years my mother knew all the students at the university

speaker: LO
words: because there were so few of them and they were fairly well integrated

speaker: LO
words: into the life of the town apparently. Being in musical circles as she was,

speaker: LO
words: she just knew everybody out of the first few graduating classesr- I don't

speaker: LO
words: imagine there*d be anybody she didn't know.

speaker: SS
words: There were some events then where they'd get together for music and that

speaker: SS
words: sort of thing.

speaker: LO
words: I think so. I think that was quite common.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know how she met your father?

speaker: LO
words: No, I really don't. She was very young, that is she must have been,

speaker: LO
words: well she was not quite eighteen when she was married, and I don't know

speaker: LO
words: how long she had known Dad, but he probably was working here in town

speaker: LO
words: and the town was probably small enough that everybody knew everybody

speaker: LO
words: else. I don't know beyond that.

speaker: SS
words: Then after they got married they stayed here for some time?

speaker: LO
words: Yes, they lived here for quite some time, then I think Father went up

speaker: LO
words: to Wallace and they lived there for a little while. And then they lived

speaker: LO
words: in Lewiston for a short time when my brother was a baby, when he was

speaker: LO
words: very young, and then came back here. Dad was kind of the auditor at one

speaker: LO
words: time—I don't know exactly when, and he'd been in the abstract business.

speaker: LO
words: I think that's what he was doing in Wallace and also in Lewiston. And

speaker: LO
words: then they lived here. I was born here,apparently by that time they were

speaker: LO
words: settled down here.

speaker: SS
words: Then your father had this s»rt of wanderlust for farming?

speaker: LO
words: Yea, I think he was a frustrated farmer because he went up to Canada

speaker: LO
words: later on and farmed a big ranch up there for Dr. Gritman. Dr. Gritman,

speaker: LO
words: I guess, was making quite a bit of money and wanted to put it to work and

speaker: LO
words: this was one of his ventures, was this farming in Canada. He had quite

speaker: LO
words: a big ranch up there near Champion in Alberta. Dad went up there several

speaker: LO
words: years and farmed for him. I suppose by that time I was in high school—

speaker: LO
words: maybe not—yea, proably I was in high school by that time.

speaker: SS
words: Well, so he would go up and farm part of the year and then come back down.

speaker: LO
words: Yes, that was it. Then later he farm^for himself in Saskatchewan for

speaker: LO
words: several seasons. I think that he sold out up in there about 1926, as

speaker: LO
words: I remember, that was about the time—'25 or '26.

speaker: SS
words: Funny, his family seemed to kave moved around a lot too,as I read some of

speaker: SS
words: the materials that he dictated to you, they seemed to have been in a lot

speaker: SS
words: of places.

speaker: LO
words: Um hum. Yes his father had farmed near Genes ee, and then he had a

speaker: LO
words: store at Uniontown, and then he had a farm down on the breaks of the

speaker: LO
words: Snake near Uniontown, and then moved back to Moscow. And he had come from

speaker: LO
words: Montana in 1876. They had moved from Montana—they went down through, oh

speaker: LO
words: by Henry's Lake and down through south Idaho and around and up to get to

speaker: LO
words: Moscow, to get to this part of the country So that was quite a little trek

speaker: LO
words: in itself.

speaker: SS
words: Were you here when you were a kid in Moscow? Most of your growing up. . .

speaker: LO
words: Um hum. Except for the couple of years that we spent in California I spent

speaker: LO
words: all my life here until I was out of college.

speaker: SS
words: : tell me about that trip when you went down to California and Mexico?

speaker: LO
words: Well, we went to California because of my mother's health, and while he

speaker: LO
words: was down there somebody sold him on the idea that he could, I suppose, homestead

speaker: LO
words: and raise wheat in New Mexico. So we all went off to a little place called

speaker: LO
words: Playas in New Mexico which was right down near the Mexican border. It was

speaker: LO
words: just, oh such a very small place. There wasn't anything there but a store.

speaker: LO
words: I don't even remember a gas station—maybe there was one, but the main line

speaker: LO
words: of the Southern Pacific came through there, it didn't stop at Plafcs unless

speaker: LO
words: you telegraphed ahead that you were going to flag it, and then it would

speaker: LO
words: stop and pick up passengers or it would let off paying passengers, but

speaker: LO
words: it picked up the mail by hooking it off a pole and tossed off mailbags.

speaker: LO
words: We were down there only a few months because it was at the time of the

speaker: LO
words: Mexican border trouble, and the week that we arrived there the postal

speaker: LO
words: service had held some kind of meeting of postmasters in that area, and

speaker: LO
words: they had gone into a town called Columbus, New Mexico. Pancho Vi'a's

speaker: LO
words: bandits had crossed the border and raided the town and had taken some of

speaker: LO
words: conventioneering postmasters out and lined them up and shot them. And

speaker: LO
words: this was all the talk just about the time we arrived. And the postmaster

speaker: LO
words: -from Plias had been there and was killed. Then later, after what I think

speaker: LO
words: probably was three or four months only, we left there very hurriedly because

speaker: LO
words: someone came riding in to our yard one day and said that a young fellow

speaker: LO
words: and his wife who were ranching about, oh I think maybe it was about thirty

speaker: LO
words: miles away from us had been killed by their Mexican farmhands who had then

speaker: LO
words: taken off across the border into Mexico. And my mother decided that was

speaker: LO
words: no place for her children. So we left.

speaker: SS
words: Fast?

speaker: LO
words: We left the very next day, and Father left in about a week or two. So

speaker: LO
words: that was the end of that. But of course that was much earlier than Father

speaker: LO
words: was in Canada because this was about the time I was

speaker: LO
words: starting to school. I think I had been part of a year to school in Los

speaker: LO
words: Angeles before this, then we went back to California and I went to first

speaker: LO
words: grade again there.

speaker: SS
words: But he decided to come back from California too?

speaker: LO
words: The reason that we decided to come back from California was that the

speaker: LO
words: house on Almon Street that father Lieuallen had built had been rented

speaker: LO
words: while we were in California to the Kappa Sigma Fraternity for a fraternity

speaker: LO
words: house, and while we were gone they decided to build their own house on

speaker: LO
words: the campus. And so Dad in the meantime had gotten the idea from observing

speaker: LO
words: apartment houses in California, he had decided that he could convert that

speaker: LO
words: house on Almon Street into an apartment house. So when the Kappa Sigs

speaker: LO
words: gave it up, we came back here and Dad spent, oh what must have been six

speaker: LO
words: months or so anyhow, remodeling it into apartments, and that was really

speaker: LO
words: the first apartment house in Moscow. It was, I think, the first one to

speaker: LO
words: have electric ranges and to have shower-baths and folding beds—beds that

speaker: LO
words: folded down out of the wall. This was quite the thing. The waiting list

speaker: LO
words: to get apartments there—there was always a waiting list in the early

speaker: LO
words: times, up until the time of the depression.

speaker: SS
words: Who were the people that wanted to stay there?

speaker: LO
words: Oh, we had bankers and physicians and university professors.

speaker: SS
words: Was this supposed to be the newest thing in living?

speaker: LO
words: Well yes. It was innovative; this was just new for Moscow.

speaker: SS
words: You mentioned to me before about the built-in kitchen that he had. Besides

speaker: SS
words: the stove were there other thijgs that he added?

speaker: LO
words: Well, a kitchen cabinet. And this was a combination cupboard, and it

speaker: LO
words: was a free-standing piece of furniture—it wasn't really built in, but it

speaker: LO
words: was a combination cupboard and breakfast table. It was a compact

speaker: LO
words: of unit. There was a place for supplies and dishes and the counter pulled

speaker: LO
words: out to make a place that you could eat, you know, like a breakfast

speaker: LO
words: table. And had a built in flour storage place with a sifter right at the

speaker: LO
words: bottom so that you could load a sack of flour into it and 'sift it right on

speaker: LO
words: out of there--you didn't have to scoop it out. And it had, oh, bread

speaker: LO
words: drawer, silverware drawer, all this sort of thing.

speaker: SS
words: Did he do the remodeling himself on these places?

speaker: LO
words: Oh, he did quite a bit of it. He hired carpenters and plumbers—he was

speaker: LO
words: quite good atthat sort of thing—he was good with his hands. And I'm sure

speaker: LO
words: that he did the designing of it, but he hired carpenters to work on it.

speaker: SS
words: Do you have any idea of what kind of rent he was gettting in those days?

speaker: LO
words: Oh, I suppose, well I think the most expensive apartment was the ground

speaker: LO
words: floor apartment. We lived on the ground floor and there was one other

speaker: LO
words: ground floor apartment, and I think that rented for fifty dollars. And I'm

speaker: LO
words: sure that was the most expensive one.

speaker: SS
words: You know there's a story about your mother that I remember you telling

speaker: SS
words: her

speaker: SS
words: before and that was about playing with the gold.

speaker: LO
words: Oh yes, about playing with the sack of twenty dollar gold pieces.

speaker: LO
words: She didn't have very toys, I suppose, and so at least once, maybe only

speaker: LO
words: once, she was given this sack of twenty dollar gold pieces to play with.

speaker: LO
words: And at the end of the day when they checked up they found that one was

speaker: LO
words: missing. And they asked her about it and she said, "Yes." And she took

speaker: LO
words: them out and showed them where she had planted it. She knew about planting

speaker: LO
words: seeds and growing more, and so she planted this twenty dollar gold piece

speaker: LO
words: in the hope of getting more. So they dug up the twenty dollar gold piece.

speaker: LO
words: I suppose you know that the streets Almon, Asbury and Lieuallen are

speaker: LO
words: named for my grandfather—that was his name—Almon Asbury Lieuallen

speaker: LO
words: And Lilly is named for my mother, but through some error the streets were

speaker: LO
words: supposed to be in order—Almon, Asbury, Lieuallen and then Lilly, but

speaker: LO
words: somehow in drawing up the plat they got them in the wrong order, so they

speaker: LO
words: run Almon, Asbury, Lilly, and then Lieuallen —in that order.

speaker: SS
words: Well, that's not too bad if it's Almon, Asbury and Lilly Lieuallen.

speaker: SS
words: That's really quite nice.

speaker: LO
words: Yes, I thought that was kind of interesting.

speaker: SS
words: Do you know anything about Noah Lieuallen.

speaker: LO
words: No, I don't, not anything like firsthand. I know that he was a schoolteacher

speaker: LO
words: here and that he was probably related to Mother. He probably could have

speaker: LO
words: been an uncle—I just don't know. But there was a big family of the

speaker: LO
words: Lieuallens down around Athena and Weston and quite a few of them

speaker: LO
words: came up here too. And I know that Mother had Lieuallen cousins and so on,

speaker: LO
words: but I just don't about Noah Lieuallen.

speaker: SS
words: What about how your father got to know so many of the oldtimers and

speaker: SS
words: the old happenings, like he seemed to know,from what you told me, quite a

speaker: SS
words: bit about about how the university got started—he personally knew Willis

speaker: SS
words: Sweet and those people?

speaker: LO
words: Well, I think that he was just here early, he and his family must have

speaker: LO
words: come in about '76 or '7 and he grew up in the area and then became a

speaker: LO
words: young businessman and interested in politics and so on. And I think he

speaker: LO
words: just knew everybody that there was locally. And he knew Willis Sweet, and

speaker: LO
words: he knew oh a lot of the prominent early men like that. After all, Moscow

speaker: LO
words: was pretty small and I think that a man in business like my father probably

speaker: LO
words: did know all the other businessmen and all the politicians.

speaker: SS
words: You told me he was a staunch Republican?

speaker: LO
words: He was raised a Democrat—he was raised by the grandfather that didn't like

speaker: LO
words: the Black Republican cherry . And it was a great source of sorrow to my

speaker: LO
words: grandfather when Dad turned out to be a backsliding Democrat. And when he

speaker: LO
words: from

speaker: LO
words: converted being a Democrat to a Republican, he became one of the

speaker: LO
words: most reactionary conservative. Republicans that you could hope to find anywhere.

speaker: LO
words: We used to tease him about belonging to the Neanderthal wing of the

speaker: LO
words: Republican party. And I asked my mother at one time if there ever was a

speaker: LO
words: president of either party that Dad approved of and she said, "No, none of

speaker: LO
words: them suited him."They all did most things wrong." No, he was really very,

speaker: LO
words: very conservative, once he quit being a Democrat.

speaker: SS
words: What would that mean? I mean can you think of any particulars that he

speaker: SS
words: felt very conservative about?

speaker: LO
words: Well, I think he was kind of all for big business. He felt that business

speaker: LO
words: was what had built this country, and thatpusinessmen deserved quite a bit

speaker: LO
words: of credit and consideration and so on. And he used to get quite upset when

speaker: LO
words: my brother and I would advance the idea that we didn't think that business

speaker: LO
words: was that much entitled to special privileges and so on. He just was not

speaker: LO
words: a man who had liberal ideas.

speaker: SS
words: Well, I guess you and your brother picked up some when you were young.

speaker: SS
words: Those ideas that business wasn't entitled to special privilege were

speaker: SS
words: ideas that you absorbed then.

speaker: LO
words: Well, yes. I think that we, maybe when we grew up and got away from

speaker: LO
words: Father's influence and had little chance to look around for ourselves that

speaker: LO
words: we saw things a little bit differently. Maybe it was the generation gap,

speaker: LO
words: but. . .

speaker: SS
words: Well, if he hated all the presidents do you think that was mostly 'cause

speaker: SS
words: he didn't like the federal government?

speaker: LO
words: No, I don't think he hated them all, but I think he just felt that they

speaker: LO
words: could have done things differently. And I think : he probably felt

speaker: LO
words: that Hoover was one of the better presidents that we had. He disliked

speaker: LO
words: Roosevelt very much. He just thought Roosevelt was terrible.

speaker: SS
words: It's funny fron what I've heard, it seems like a number of Democrats

speaker: SS
words: became Republicans during Cleveland's time. I think that 1893 depression

speaker: SS
words: turned quite a few.

speaker: LO
words: That might have had something to do with it. I never did know what it

speaker: LO
words: was particularly that caused him to change.
